title: Numerical Tricks of the Trade
tagline: Log-sum-exp, log-softmax, BCE-with-logits, and the fixes that keep floating point honest
summary: Float64 overflows at $e^{700}$. Float32 overflows at $e^{88}$. Neural networks routinely compute quantities that blow past both limits. This page shows the four most important numerical tricks in ML — log-sum-exp, log-softmax, BCE-with-logits, and log1p/expm1 — as side-by-side naive-breaks / stable-works comparisons.
highlights: Slide a single logit up to 700 and watch the naive softmax compute +Infinity while the stable version returns the correct answer.
See why log(softmax(x)) is the wrong composition and log_softmax(x) exists as a single fused operation.
Watch BCE-with-logits stay finite at logit magnitudes of ±200 while the naive BCE returns NaN.
Drag x down to 1e-10 and see log(1 - exp(-x)) lose 6 digits of precision — rescued by log1p and expm1.
Reference table of 10 more stability tricks: Welford's variance, lgamma, scaled L2 norms, and more.
